pricing.source: OpenAI API pricing, standard short-context rates, 2026-06-24

hardChecks.label: Citation source ids
hardChecks.expectation: Every cited source id exists in the source packet

hardChecks.label: Cost metadata
hardChecks.expectation: estimatedUsd is present when pricing is configured

hardChecks.label: Token metadata
hardChecks.expectation: Input, cached-input, and output token counts are coherent

hardChecks.label: Provider and model metadata
hardChecks.expectation: Provider, model, and prompt version are present

hardChecks.label: Judge input boundary
hardChecks.expectation: Judge prompt omits eval-only object keys and holdout tuning fields

hardChecks.label: Judge claim coverage
hardChecks.expectation: LLM judge returns exactly one judgment for every briefing claim

claimJudgments.claimText: Current generated release notes cover most merged work (83%), but 14% of entries were inaccurate because behavior changed after final QA; the riskiest errors came from stale pull-request descriptions after scope cuts.
claimJudgments.explanation: S1 states generated notes covered 83% of merged changes, 14% described behavior changed after final QA, and the highest-risk errors came from stale PR descriptions after scope cuts.

claimJudgments.claimText: Customer escalations can be triggered by a single inaccurate promise (e.g., an admin toggle mentioned in notes but removed before release), and customers particularly value accurate migration warnings and compatibility caveats.
claimJudgments.explanation: S2 describes one escalation caused by a generated note promising an admin toggle removed before release, and says customers value concise notes especially migration warnings and compatibility caveats, with accuracy emphasized.

claimJudgments.claimText: QA changes after PR text is written include renamed features, a removed admin toggle, and a late-added migration warning—making stale PR text unreliable unless notes are compared to the QA delta log before publication.
claimJudgments.explanation: S5 lists two feature-name changes, one removed admin toggle, and one late-added migration warning, and says generated notes should compare against the QA delta log before publication because stale PR text is unreliable after scope cuts.

claimJudgments.claimText: Engineering will tag final-release deltas during QA but will not maintain a parallel/second changelog; they prefer a lightweight review queue that highlights changed or removed claims rather than full rewrites.
claimJudgments.explanation: S3 says engineers can/will tag final-release deltas during QA if lightweight, will not maintain a second/parallel changelog, and recommends a review queue highlighting changed or removed claims instead of rewriting every note.

claimJudgments.claimText: Compliance requires explicit human approval for any customer-facing note mentioning security, data retention, or access control, and wants sensitive statements to be easy to spot in the review flow.
claimJudgments.explanation: S4 explicitly requires human approval for notes mentioning security, data retention, or access control and says sensitive claims must be easy to spot before publishing.

missingImportantEvidence: S6 is not cited in the generated briefing, although it directly supports the checklist-focused recommendation to inspect changed claims, removed features, sensitive categories, compatibility warnings, and customer-commitment/migration-instruction claims without rewriting every note.
recommendationJudgment.explanation: The recommendation is well supported by S1 and S5 for checking QA deltas/stale PR drift, S3 for a lightweight review queue rather than a second changelog, S4 for explicit approval of sensitive categories, S2 for treating migration and compatibility caveats as high risk, and S6 for the release-editor checklist focus. It directly advises how to improve generated release notes while keeping customer-facing claims accurate. The main evidence gap is citation coverage: the briefing relies on the checklist concept but omits S6 from cited sources.

citationSupport.note: S1 appears in fully supported claim-level judgments.
S2 appears in fully supported claim-level judgments.
S5 appears in fully supported claim-level judgments.
S3 appears in fully supported claim-level judgments.
S4 appears in fully supported claim-level judgments.

notes: Hybrid evaluator output: deterministic hard checks plus structured LLM judge evidence. Manual spot checks are still required before claiming product improvement.
